AMAT.headline: China revenue contracted and mix shifted from foundry/logic to DRAM/HBM, masking flat-to-down top-line behind a memory-driven bottleneck rotation

shifts.trend: DRAM/HBM rose from 26% to 34% of Semiconductor Systems mix QoQ; NAND held at 4-7%; foundry/logic fell from 67% to 62%
shifts.note: Memory is absorbing the capex share that foundry/logic is giving up — HBM technology transitions are the immediate WFE demand driver

shifts.trend: China revenue $8.5B in FY25 (-16% YoY); Taiwan jumped to $1.7B (+46%) and Korea -13% in Q1; China share compressed from 37% to ~30%
shifts.note: US export controls continue to structurally reduce China WFE share; Taiwan leading-edge picks up the slack but that swap is now embedded in run-rate

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $28.4B (+4% YoY); Q1 FY26 revenue $7.0B (-2% YoY)
shifts.note: Top-line has stalled as China headwinds offset memory/HBM tailwinds; the mix rotation is happening but is not yet additive to total revenue

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 48.7% (+1.2pts YoY); Q1 FY26 gross margin 49.0% (+0.2pts YoY) with Q1 operating margin -4.3pts from $253M BIS settlement charge
shifts.note: Underlying gross margin stable; the settlement is non-recurring but reflects ongoing export-control compliance risk as a real cost

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K flags HBM advanced packaging as growth driver; Q1 FY26 10-Q discloses $253M BIS export-controls compliance settlement for prior China shipments
shifts.note: The settlement confirms AMAT's China exposure created legal liability; tightening controls are constraining both revenue and compliance costs

AMAT.verdict: Thesis intact but the China-to-leading-edge revenue swap is still incomplete — AMAT needs HBM/advanced packaging to compound further before total revenue re-accelerates; the BIS settlement is a one-time margin hit, not a structural impairment

AMD.headline: Data Center revenue accelerated from +32% to +57% YoY as MI350 GPU ramp and the 6 GW OpenAI deal drove incremental volume, and gross margins expanded 3pts to 53%

shifts.trend: Data Center revenue $16.6B in FY25 (+32% YoY); Q1 FY26 Data Center $5.8B (+57% YoY) — run-rate implies ~$23B annualized
shifts.note: The acceleration is real and demand-pull driven; OpenAI 6 GW MI450 agreement provides multi-year contracted volume that has no analog in prior AMD history

shifts.trend: FY25 consolidated gross margin 50%; Q1 FY26 gross margin 53% (+3pts YoY); Data Center segment operating margin improved from ~22% (FY25, hit by $440M MI308 China charges) to 28% in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Margin improvement is structural — richer DC mix and absence of one-time export-charge reversals; 28% DC operating margin shows GPU profitability improving as Instinct matures

shifts.trend: FY25: $800M MI308 Q2 inventory charge, $360M reversed Q4; China geo revenue 22% ($7.8B) in FY25; Q1 FY26: MI325 licenses granted with mandatory 25% US re-import tariff
shifts.note: China AI GPU access remains structurally constrained; partial MI325 licenses are a workaround, not a resolution — tariff friction adds cost for any China customer

shifts.trend: FY25: no customer >10% revenue; Q1 FY26: international revenue jumped to 74% of total (vs 66%), reflecting hyperscaler GPU shipping locations
shifts.note: AMD's revenue is becoming more concentrated in hyperscaler AI GPU orders; the OpenAI warrant structure and 6 GW agreement create a symbiotic but concentrated revenue dependency

shifts.trend: FY25: ZT Systems acquired $3.2B, manufacturing sold for $2.4B, design IP retained; Q1 FY26: R&D +39% to $2.4B and MG&A +41% to $1.3B
shifts.note: AMD is investing aggressively in headcount and R&D to sustain the annual Instinct cadence (MI350→MI355X→MI450); ZT Systems IP retention underpins the Helios rack-scale offering

AMD.verdict: Thesis strongly intact — GPU acceleration is structurally confirmed by the OpenAI deal and margin expansion; the China overhang is a manageable drag, not a thesis killer, as long as the hyperscaler demand cycle persists

AMKR.headline: Revenue inflected to +27% growth in Q1 2026 as all four end markets turned positive, but the story is the step-change in capex — from $905M in all of FY25 to a $2.5-3.0B FY26 plan driven by the Arizona advanced-packaging fab

shifts.trend: FY25 net sales $6.71B (+6.2% YoY); Q1 2026 net sales $1.68B (+27.5% YoY)
shifts.note: All four end markets grew in Q1 2026 — communications +42%, auto/industrial +28%, computing +19%, consumer +4% — a broad-based inflection after a muted FY25

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $904.6M (13.5% of sales); FY26 guided $2.5-3.0B (est. ~38% of sales); Q1 2026 capex $224.6M vs $79.9M Q1 2025
shifts.note: The capex step-change is one of the largest in AMKR history; Arizona fab (1.8M sq ft, manufacturing H1 2028) is the primary driver — this is the advanced-packaging capacity the AI hardware supply chain needs

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 14.0% (vs 14.8% FY24) — Vietnam ramp pressured; Q1 2026 gross margin 14.2% (+2.3pts YoY) on higher utilization
shifts.note: Margin inflecting as utilization improves; the Arizona ramp will initially pressure margins again when it begins, similar to Vietnam pattern

shifts.trend: FY25 Apple 29.8%, Qualcomm 11.1%, top-10 = 72% of sales; Q1 2026 customer concentration disclosed as same (72% top-10 from 2025 annual)
shifts.note: High concentration in Apple and Qualcomm persists; Arizona fab is partly a strategic bet to win datacenter/AI customers to diversify, given FCBGA/2.5D/HBM integration positioning

AMKR.verdict: Thesis structurally unlocking — AMKR is making the largest capex commitment in its history to build advanced-packaging capacity that AI hardware needs; the revenue inflection validates demand, though full Arizona capacity won't hit until 2028

AMZN.headline: AWS revenue growth accelerated from +20% to +28% YoY and capex nearly doubled QoQ to $43.2B, while the AWS RPO surged from $244B to $364B on the $100B OpenAI expansion alone

shifts.trend: AWS revenue $128.7B FY25 (+20% YoY); Q1 2026 AWS revenue $37.6B (+28% YoY)
shifts.note: The acceleration is driven by AI workload mix shift into AWS — re-acceleration from 20% to 28% on a $130B+ base is a meaningful absolute dollar addition
Q1 alone was $43B — if annualized, AMZN's 2026 capex run-rate would exceed $170B; management guided FY26 'to increase' from FY25, consistent with this trajectory

shifts.trend: FY25: RPO $244B; Q1 2026: AWS RPO $364B (vs $244B at Dec 31 2025) — driven by $100B OpenAI expansion of existing $38B deal + new Trainium chip obligations
shifts.note: RPO is no longer just cloud credits — it now includes AWS Trainium chip supply obligations, meaning compute silicon is part of the commercial construct with OpenAI

shifts.trend: AWS operating margin FY25 35.4% ($45.6B operating income on $128.7B); Q1 2026 AWS operating margin 37.7% ($14.16B on $37.6B) — margin expanding despite capex surge
shifts.note: AWS margins are still expanding even as capex doubles; implies the incremental AI workload is high-margin relative to the infrastructure cost, at least in the near term

shifts.trend: FY25 performance obligations $244B primarily AWS commitments; Q1 2026 $15B invested in OpenAI Series C + committed $35B additional (total $50B); $5B Anthropic + $20B financing facility post-quarter
shifts.note: AMZN is concentrating its AI bets on a small number of frontier AI labs; the $70B+ committed to OpenAI and Anthropic represents a strategic lock-in risk and an upside multiplier

AMZN.verdict: AWS is the inference layer everyone needs — accelerating revenue, expanding margins, and a surging RPO that now includes chip supply obligations confirms this is a demand pull, not a push; capex doubling is the signal, not the concern

ANET.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +28.6% to +35.1% YoY and deferred revenue swelled by $826M in a single quarter to $6.2B as AI Ethernet trial conversions accumulate, but gross margin slipped 180bps on large-customer mix and silicon supply pressure

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $9.0B (+28.6% YoY); Q1 FY26 revenue $2.71B (+35.1% YoY)
shifts.note: Growth is accelerating as Cloud and AI Titans (48% of FY25 revenue) increase AI infrastructure deployments; Etherlink AI Ethernet portfolio is shipping in volume

shifts.trend: FY25: deferred revenue $5.37B (up from $2.79B YE24); Q1 FY26: deferred revenue jumped another $826M to $6.20B; binding contractual backlog $968M
shifts.note: Deferred revenue near-doubled in FY25 and is still growing in Q1 FY26 — the acceptance-period contract structure means booked-but-not-recognized revenue is building a substantial buffer even if near-term shipments wobble

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 64.1%; Q1 FY26 gross margin 61.9% (-220bps YoY and -180bps vs FY25 level)
shifts.note: Memory and silicon supply tightening is explicitly cited by management as a gross margin pressure; large-customer pricing discounts on big AI Ethernet orders compound the effect

shifts.trend: FY25: two end customers were 26% and 16% of revenue; Q1 FY26: Americas rose to 84.5% of revenue (vs 79.7% prior year), reflecting US cloud titan concentration
shifts.note: Americas share rising signals even more concentration in the 2-3 largest US hyperscalers; geographic diversity is declining as AI infrastructure investment concentrates among a few players

shifts.trend: FY25: $6.1B total RPO, 90% within 2 years; Q1 FY26: $7.7B total RPO, 91% within 2 years
shifts.note: Management warns near-term revenue may not reflect demand levels because shipments run against pre-committed deployment plans — this is supply-chain staging language, not demand caution

ANET.verdict: AI Ethernet trials are converting and the deferred revenue build is a positive leading indicator, but the 180bps gross margin compression from silicon supply and large-customer mix is the key risk to watch — if it stabilizes, the thesis is intact; if it widens, it becomes a structural issue

AVGO.headline: Semiconductor revenue surged +52% YoY in Q1 FY26 on custom AI accelerators, and customer concentration intensified sharply — the top-five end customers jumped from ~40% to ~50% of revenue and one distributor went from 29% to 42% of total revenue

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $63.9B (+24% YoY); Q1 FY26 revenue $19.3B (+29% YoY); Q1 FY26 Semiconductor solutions $12.5B (+52% YoY)
shifts.note: The semiconductor segment grew 52% YoY driven entirely by custom AI accelerators (XPUs) and AI networking; this is not a broad semi cyclical recovery — it is AI-specific demand

shifts.trend: FY25 top-five end customers ~40% of revenue; Q1 FY26 top-five ~50% of revenue; one distributor rose from 29% to 42% of Q1 revenue
shifts.note: One hyperscaler XPU customer is effectively the marginal revenue driver at AVGO; the distributor concentration at 42% of quarterly revenue is extreme and creates binary risk on that relationship

shifts.trend: FY25 Infrastructure software 42% of revenue ($27.0B); Q1 FY26 Infrastructure software $6.8B (+1% YoY), now only 35% of mix as semiconductor re-rates
shifts.note: VMware/software integration is complete and generating stable revenue, but AI semi is growing so fast that it is naturally diluting software's share — a mix shift that compresses software's blended premium

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 68%; Q1 FY26 gross margin 68% (flat YoY) despite higher semiconductor mix
shifts.note: Gross margin held flat at 68% despite semi growing faster than software; AI semi margins are high enough to offset the usual software-premium dilution, validating XPU custom design premium

shifts.trend: FY25 China shipments 17% of revenue (vs 20% FY24); no new China-specific disclosures in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: China exposure already declining; AVGO's custom XPU model means most China-destination AI silicon would be restricted anyway, so this is less a risk than for GPU vendors

AVGO.verdict: The AI custom ASIC thesis is at full velocity — 52% semiconductor growth and one hyperscaler driving 40%+ of revenue quarter means AVGO is the concentrated beneficiary of at least one very large AI buildout; concentration is the risk, but it's also why the upside is real

CAT.headline: Power & Energy segment sustained strong growth momentum with P&E revenues +22% in Q1 2026 and robust backlog growth continuing, even as tariff costs ($2.2-2.4B FY26 expected) pressured margins

shifts.trend: FY25 Power & Energy sales $32.2B (+12%); Power Generation sub-segment $10.3B (+32%); Q1 FY26 P&E revenues $7.0B (+22% YoY)
shifts.note: Power Generation has maintained 20%+ growth in both periods driven by large reciprocating engines for data center applications — this is a multi-year order book converting to revenue

shifts.trend: FY25 backlog $51.2B (vs $30.0B at YE24, +71%); Q1 FY26 backlog growth driven by both Power Generation and Oil & Gas — sequential backlog commentary remains positive
shifts.note: A $51B backlog nearly doubling in one year is a structural demand signal, not a cyclical wiggle; the data-center-driven prime power orders are now explicitly called out in both filings

shifts.trend: FY25 P&E margin 19.9% (flat YoY); Q1 FY26 consolidated operating margin 17.7% (vs 18.1%); adjusted operating margin 18.0% (vs 18.3%)
shifts.note: Tariff costs ($2.2-2.4B FY26) are the primary margin pressure — manufacturing cost inflation from tariffs is offsetting the volume leverage from strong backlog conversion

shifts.trend: FY25: tariff impact embedded in FY25 cost structure; Q1 FY26: SCOTUS ruled IEEPA tariffs unauthorized but $1.0B Q1 costs incurred and refund 'not deemed probable'; Q2 expected ~$700M tariff costs
shifts.note: The SCOTUS ruling creates uncertainty but the accounting conservatism (no refund accrual) suggests CAT is managing tariff exposure as a real cost; the $2.2-2.4B FY26 estimate remains the working assumption

CAT.verdict: The data center prime power thesis is intact — backlog tripling and Power Generation growing 20-32% confirms structural demand — but tariffs are a real margin headwind that dilutes the earnings leverage from the revenue acceleration

CDNS.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +14% to +19% YoY in Q1 FY26 as China rebounded +36% and the RPO backlog grew to $8.0B, with operating margins holding at 29% despite ongoing trade-control uncertainty

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $5,297M (+14% YoY); Q1 FY26 revenue $1,474M (+19% YoY); China revenue $680M FY25 (+19%) → $189M Q1 FY26 (+36% YoY)
shifts.note: The China re-acceleration is notable given the May-July 2025 BIS 'is-informed' license whiplash; rescission allowed backlog to convert and pulled demand forward into Q1 FY26

shifts.trend: FY25 RPO backlog $7.8B (53% recognized in next 12mo); Q1 FY26 RPO $8.0B (55% in next 12mo)
shifts.note: RPO is growing modestly and the current-year recognition share is increasing — a healthy signal that the pipeline is converting, not just stacking

shifts.trend: FY25 operating margin 28% (pressured by $128.5M BIS/DOJ settlement); Q1 FY26 operating margin 29% (matching prior-year Q1)
shifts.note: Excluding the one-time settlement, FY25 margins would have been ~31%; Q1 FY26 holding at 29% signals underlying margin structure is healthy

shifts.trend: FY25: BIS imposed EDA license requirement May 23, 2025 then rescinded July 2, 2025; Q1 FY26: company notes 'limited' impact from current expanded trade controls but flags Sep 2025 BIS 50%-owned-affiliate rule
shifts.note: The regulatory headline is net-positive (rescission held through Q1 FY26) but the affiliate rule creates a new vector for future restrictions — this is an ongoing binary regulatory risk for EDA-China revenue

shifts.trend: FY25 repurchased 3.2M shares for $925M; Q1 FY26 repurchased 671K shares for $200M (vs $350M Q1 FY25); revolving credit drawn for Hexagon D&E acquisition
shifts.note: Capital allocation shifted from buybacks to the Hexagon D&E acquisition (MSC Nastran, Adams) — near-term buyback pace reduced as debt is deployed for strategic expansion

CDNS.verdict: Thesis intact — EDA backlog is compounding at 19%+ and China is re-accelerating, but the BIS affiliate rule creates a new overhang; the Hexagon acquisition is a bet on simulation software demand in AI hardware design, which is consistent with the inference stack thesis

CEG.headline: Constellation closed the $22B Calpine acquisition in January 2026, transforming into the world's largest private power producer at 55 GW — the data-center nuclear PPA story graduated to a vertically integrated clean power platform

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: 20-year Meta PPA for Clinton signed, Calpine merger approved by FERC/PUCT/NYPSC; FY25 10-K: Calpine closed Jan 7, 2026 for ~$22B adding 23 GW (21 GW gas, 730 MW geothermal, 800 MW battery)
shifts.note: The Calpine acquisition is the largest strategic move — it adds dispatchable natural gas to complement nuclear baseload, directly serving data centers that need always-on power

shifts.trend: FY25 operating revenues $25.5B vs $23.6B FY24; Q3 2025 GAAP net income $930M vs $1.2B Q3 2024; FY25 adjusted EPS $9.39 vs $8.67
shifts.note: Core financial performance growing modestly; the GAAP decline is a nuclear PTC timing artifact, not an operating deterioration — adjusted EPS actually grew

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: Crane Clean Energy Center (TMI, 835 MW) in DOE loan guarantee process; FY25: $1.0B DOE loan guarantee received Nov 2025; NY PSC approved 20-year ZEC extension Jan 2026
shifts.note: Regulatory infrastructure is being put in place to sustain and expand the nuclear fleet; the ZEC extension to 2049 removes near-term retirement risk for New York plants

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: FY26 capex guidance not yet disclosed; FY25 10-K: $22B Calpine cash+stock consideration; $1.0B DOE loan guarantee; intent to file 80-year license extensions across nuclear fleet
shifts.note: The combined company will require significant investment to integrate Calpine and pursue nuclear license extensions — the capital allocation is shifting from buybacks to infrastructure build

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: majority of revenue from nuclear PTC/ZECs + PPAs; FY25: post-Calpine, 2.5M retail accounts including 75% of Fortune 100 — customer base and power mix diversified
shifts.note: Calpine's retail load (60 TWh) and Fortune 100 customer relationships provide diversification away from nuclear-PTC-dependent revenue, reducing regulatory concentration risk

CEG.verdict: The thesis materially upgraded — CEG is no longer just a nuclear PPA play for data centers; Calpine makes it the go-to dispatchable clean power platform, which unlocks a broader and larger set of hyperscaler power contracts than nuclear alone could serve

CLS.headline: Revenue growth jumped from +42% (FY25 CCS) to +76% (Q1 FY26 CCS) YoY as both hyperscaler networking switch programs and the previously-paused AI/ML compute program ramped simultaneously

shifts.trend: FY25 CCS segment +42% to $9.19B; Communications end-market +81%; Q1 FY26 revenue $4.05B (+53% YoY); CCS +76% to $3.24B; Communications +69%
shifts.note: Both HPS networking switch ramps and the next-gen AI/ML compute program ramp contributed — the Enterprise end market went from -19% in FY25 to +101% in Q1 FY26 as the paused program restarted

shifts.trend: FY25 HPS revenue $5.0B (+81%), 41% of total; Q1 FY26 HPS ~$1.7B (+63%), 42% of total — plus Enterprise +101% on next-gen AI/ML compute program ramp
shifts.note: HPS (hyperscaler networking switch programs) is the core AI infrastructure bottleneck exposure; the enterprise AI/ML compute reboot is additional upside that was not in FY25 numbers

shifts.trend: FY25: 3 CCS customers each >10% (32%, 14%, 12%); Q1 FY26 10-Q does not re-disclose but implies same hyperscaler concentration drives 80% CCS share
shifts.note: Extremely concentrated in 2-3 hyperscaler customers — this is the feature (execution visibility) and the bug (customer-termination or program-change risk)

shifts.trend: FY25 capex guided $1B (~6% of revenue); Q1 FY26: revolver upsized from $750M to $1.75B and Term A loan refinanced to April 2031, expanding capacity for hyperscaler-driven ramp
shifts.note: Credit facility expansion signals management confidence in the multi-year nature of the ramp; capex deployment in Thailand, US, Mexico, Japan confirms geographic diversification of manufacturing

shifts.trend: FY25 Q4 gross margin 11.8%; Q1 FY26 CCS segment margin 8.6% (vs 8.0% Q1 FY25); adjusted EPS $2.16 beat high end of $1.95-2.15 guide
shifts.note: CMS margins thin by nature but expanding on operating leverage; beat vs guidance suggests volume upside is flowing through to the bottom line

CLS.verdict: This is the clearest AI infrastructure supply-chain unlock in the dataset — CLS is the EMS layer for hyperscaler networking switch programs and its revenue acceleration confirms those programs are ramping hard; concentration is the one structural risk

CMI.headline: Power Systems EBITDA margin expanded from 18.4% to 29.5% as data-center-driven demand accelerated with 6-8 quarter backlog visibility confirmed across both filings

shifts.trend: FY25 Power Systems EBITDA $1.69B (22.7% margin, vs 18.4% FY24); Q1 FY26 Power Systems EBITDA $577M (29.5% margin vs 23.6% Q1 FY25)
shifts.note: A 690bps margin expansion in a single quarter signals that pricing power and operating leverage are simultaneously present; data center customers are willing to pay a premium for supply certainty

shifts.trend: FY25 Power Systems sales $7.46B (+16%); Power Generation $4.73B (+19%); Q1 FY26 Power Systems sales $1.96B (+19% YoY); Power Generation $1.28B (+28%)
shifts.note: Power Generation (the data center subsegment) is growing faster than Power Systems overall and re-accelerated to +28% in Q1 from +19% in FY25 full year

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: data center backlog extending 6-8 quarters out; Q1 FY26 10-Q: Power Systems strong demand outlook maintained including power generation and industrial
shifts.note: 6-8 quarter backlog is consistent across both filings — this is a multi-year demand pull, not a one-quarter surge

shifts.trend: FY25 consolidated revenue $33.7B (-1% YoY) dragged by truck demand; Q1 FY26 worldwide revenues +3% with international +16% (China and Europe power generation); US/Canada -6% on weaker truck demand
shifts.note: Truck weakness is a cyclical offset; the Power Systems data center contribution is structural and insulated from the truck cycle — investors should separate these segments analytically

CMI.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — Power Generation margin expansion to 29.5% is the best evidence that CMI has genuine pricing power in the data center backup power market; 6-8 quarter backlog makes this a 2026-2027 earnings story with high visibility

COHR.headline: D&C segment revenue growth accelerated from +34% to +41% YoY, gross margin expanded 100bps to 38%, and management introduced 'scale across' AI interconnect as a new demand category alongside the existing DCI and datacenter transceivers

shifts.trend: FQ2 26 D&C revenue $1.21B (+34% YoY); FQ3 26 D&C revenue $1.36B (+41% YoY); consolidated revenue $1.69B (FQ2) → $1.81B (FQ3), +17% and +21% respectively
shifts.note: The D&C segment acceleration is real; +7pts improvement in YoY growth in a single quarter on an already-high base

shifts.trend: FQ2 26 D&C segment profit +44% to $306M, consolidated gross margin +140bps; FQ3 26 D&C segment profit +49% to $348M, consolidated gross margin +243bps YoY to 38%
shifts.note: Yield improvements and pricing optimization in transceivers are structural — the mix shift toward higher-value AI datacenter optics is re-rating COHR's margin profile

shifts.trend: FQ2 26 Industrial segment -10% on aerospace divestiture; FQ3 26 Industrial -16% on Munich and aerospace divestitures; portfolio is narrowing toward AI/DC optics
shifts.note: FQ3 introduced 'scale across' interconnect as a third category alongside DCI and traditional telecom — this is the first explicit naming of inter-datacenter AI fabric as a growth vector

shifts.trend: FQ2 26: no specific debt changes; FQ3 26 interest expense -$13M YoY on term-loan paydown; $14M equity-investment gain added to EPS
shifts.note: Balance sheet deleveraging is happening in parallel with the optics ramp — improving the quality of earnings as interest costs decline

COHR.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating — the 'scale across' category addition in FQ3 confirms the next leg of optics demand (inter-DC AI fabric) is real, not just a continuation of existing DCI; COHR's portfolio transformation toward AI optics is on track and margins are expanding

CRM.headline: Full-year revenue of $41.5B grew 10% and RPO surged from $59.5B (Q3) to $72.4B (year-end) as Agentforce branding consolidated AI/CRM and the $9.6B Informatica acquisition added agentic data management

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 revenue $10.26B (+9% YoY); FY26 full-year revenue $41.5B (+10% YoY); Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack/Other +19% in Q3 — AI/consumption bucket the only double-digit segment
shifts.note: Growth stable at 10% — not accelerating despite AI investment; the Agentforce rebranding is marketing-heavy; whether it drives revenue inflection depends on consumption-pricing uptake

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 RPO $59.5B (down from $63.4B at Jan 2025); FY26 10-K total RPO $72.4B (+14% YoY); current RPO $35.1B (+16% YoY)
shifts.note: The Q3 RPO decline was an anomaly — the FY26 year-end RPO re-accelerated above prior-year levels; cRPO growing faster than total RPO (+16% vs +14%) is a positive near-term demand signal

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 operating margin implied ~18% from available data; FY26 operating margin expanded to 20% from 19%; OCF $15.0B (+15%)
shifts.note: Margin expansion in a year of heavy AI investment and a $9.6B acquisition is notable — restructuring efficiency is flowing through even as AI products require investment

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26: RPO $59.5B, cash $11.3B (declined from $14.0B); FY26: repurchased $12.7B stock + $1.6B dividends = $14.3B returned; OCF $15.0B
shifts.note: CRM returned nearly 100% of operating cash flow to shareholders in FY26 while funding a $9.6B acquisition — the Informatica deal was partially debt-funded

CRM.verdict: Thesis for the AI software layer is intact but the growth rate is not accelerating; Agentforce consumption-pricing upside is real but unproven at scale — CRM is a compounder at 10% growth + buybacks, not yet an accelerator on AI agent monetization

CRWV.headline: Revenue grew +112% YoY in Q1 2026 as RPO surged from $60.7B to $98.8B, while Microsoft's revenue concentration fell from 67% to 45% as Meta emerged at 20% — customer diversification is happening at scale

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $5.1B (+168% YoY); Q1 2026 revenue $2.08B (+112% YoY); Q1 annualized implies ~$8.3B run rate
shifts.note: Growth is slowing from the FY25 base effect but the absolute revenue addition is accelerating — $2.08B in one quarter vs $5.1B for all of FY25 shows the ramp is still in full swing

shifts.trend: FY25 RPO $60.7B at Dec 31 2025 (5-year avg duration); Q1 2026 RPO $98.8B at Mar 31 2026 — 63% increase in one quarter
shifts.note: The RPO surge to $98.8B in one quarter is the defining data point — CRWV is booking multi-year compute capacity commitments faster than it can recognize revenue; supply is the constraint, not demand

shifts.trend: FY25: Microsoft 67% of revenue; Q1 2026: Microsoft (Customer A) 45%, Meta (Customer B) 20%; Microsoft declined from 72% Q1 2025 to 45% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Customer diversification is real and rapid — Microsoft going from 67% to 45% in one year as Meta grows to 20% is the most important strategic development; reduces binary customer risk

shifts.trend: FY25 total debt $21.6B (effective rates 9-15%); Q1 2026 post-quarter capital raise: $4.0B 1.75% converts + $2.8B 9.75% senior notes + $1.0B equity + repaid $1.5B revolver
shifts.note: Post-quarter capital raise shows the debt stack is being actively refinanced — the 1.75% converts replace high-yield exposure, but the 9.75% senior notes remain expensive; total debt will exceed $25B

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $10.3B; Q1 2026: committed up to $1.2B in two new data center JV equity stakes; 850 MW active → 3.1 GW total contracted power
shifts.note: The infrastructure build rate is accelerating through JV structures to share capital risk while preserving operational control; 3.1 GW contracted vs 850 MW active implies a 3.6x active-to-contracted gap

CRWV.verdict: Thesis intact and de-risking — the customer diversification toward Meta alongside the RPO surge to $98.8B resolves the binary Microsoft concentration risk that was the primary concern; the debt stack is the remaining overhang but is being actively managed

CSCO.headline: Networking growth accelerated from +15% to +21% YoY driven by AI Infrastructure and Data Center Switching, with first-half product revenue +12% confirming cloud titan order momentum is sustained and not just a pull-forward

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 Networking +15% (+$1.0B); Q2 FY26 Networking +21% (+$1.4B); 1H FY26 Networking +18% (+$2.5B); total revenue $14.88B → $15.35B sequential quarters
shifts.note: The Networking acceleration from 15% to 21% in consecutive quarters is the key signal — AI Infrastructure and Data Center Switching are compounding, not plateauing

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26: inventory +7% and purchase commitments +10% vs FYE 2025 for Silicon One manufacturing; Q2 FY26: ongoing Silicon One purchase commitment buildup for hyperscalers
shifts.note: CSCO is locking in Silicon One capacity with contract manufacturers to ensure supply for hyperscaler orders — this supply-chain pre-commitment raises their obsolescence risk but confirms demand visibility

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 Security -4% on Splunk on-prem to cloud transition; Q2 FY26 Security -4% with trend expected to continue in 2H FY26
shifts.note: Security decline offsets some of the Networking strength; the Splunk cloud transition is a structural mix shift that will depress reported Security revenue for several more quarters

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 product GM -0.6pts on mix/pricing; Q2 FY26 product GM +0.2pts on productivity; overall EPS +31% Q2 driven by tax rate improvement (12.9% vs 15.9%)
shifts.note: Product margins fluctuating within a narrow band; the EPS growth is being amplified by tax-rate tailwinds, not operating leverage alone

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26: $2.0B buybacks + $1.6B dividend; RPO $42.87B slightly below year-end; Q2 FY26: consistent capital return cadence maintained
shifts.note: Capital returns are consistent and stable; CSCO is not sacrificing the dividend or buyback to fund AI infrastructure — balance sheet at $15.7B cash vs $28.1B total debt

CSCO.verdict: The networking thesis is intact and accelerating — AI Infrastructure orders from cloud titans are real and driving sequential Networking growth; the Security drag is a known transition, not a structural problem, but it moderates the total revenue growth picture

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas backlog accelerated from +31% to +44% YoY and Q1 orders grew +42% organic as data center demand intensified, even as the pending $9.5B Boyd Thermal acquisition will add liquid cooling to the portfolio

shifts.trend: FY25 Electrical Americas backlog $13.2B (+31% YoY, +19% organic); Q1 FY26 Electrical Americas backlog $14.5B (+44% YoY, +32% organic); total backlog $19.8B at YE25
shifts.note: Backlog growth is accelerating: +31% in FY25 to +44% YoY by Q1 FY26 — implies the order intake rate is outpacing delivery, which means backlog duration is extending and visibility is lengthening

shifts.trend: FY25 Electrical Americas sales $13.3B (+16%); Q1 FY26 Electrical Americas sales $3.60B (+20%); Electrical Global Q1 sales $1.95B (+21%)
shifts.note: Revenue growth is re-accelerating in Q1 FY26 as backlog converts; both Americas and Global segments are now growing 20%+ organically on data center strength

shifts.trend: FY25 Electrical Americas operating margin 29.9% (vs 30.2%); Q1 FY26 Electrical Americas operating margin 25.6% (vs 30.0%) — 480bps decline
shifts.note: Commodity and wage inflation is compressing margins even as volume surges; 480bps deterioration in one quarter is significant and driven by input cost inflation, not pricing power erosion — pricing is holding with 210bps of leverage visible

shifts.trend: FY25: $9.5B Boyd Thermal acquisition signed (liquid cooling for hyperscale/colo); Fibrebond (modular DC), Resilient (solid-state transformers) acquired in 2025; Q1 FY26: Boyd Thermal closing pending
shifts.note: The Boyd Thermal acquisition is the strategic pivot — adding liquid cooling (chip-to-grid) to the existing electrical portfolio creates a more complete data center infrastructure offering

ETN.verdict: Thesis intact and expanding — the backlog acceleration to +44% YoY and Boyd Thermal acquisition confirm ETN is transitioning from electrical infrastructure play to a comprehensive data center power-and-cooling platform; the margin compression is a near-term tariff/inflation hit, not structural

FN.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +35.9% to +39.3% YoY as DCI revenue nearly doubled (+90.4%) and telecom surged +42.5%, while Datacom/intra-DC stagnated — the 1.6T cycle is real and FN is executing on both the speed-upgrade and the inter-DC AI scale-across wave

shifts.trend: FQ2 FY26 revenue $1.13B (+35.9% YoY); FQ3 FY26 revenue $1.21B (+39.3% YoY); 9M revenue $3.33B (+32.5%)
shifts.note: The growth rate is accelerating in consecutive quarters; DCI at +90.4% YoY is the fastest-growing line and represents inter-datacenter AI fabric demand — a new category vs traditional transceiver upgrades

shifts.trend: FQ2: DCI $142.2M (new category call-out); FQ3: DCI $196.9M (+90.4% YoY); FQ2 Telecom $412.2M; FQ3 Telecom $431.4M (+42.5% YoY); Datacom (intra-DC) essentially flat both quarters
shifts.note: The shift from Datacom to DCI within optical revenue mix confirms the AI scale-across thesis — AI clusters need inter-DC connectivity as much as intra-DC, and 1.6T upgrade is driving both

shifts.trend: FQ2 Non-optical $300.3M (+61.1% YoY, HPC $85.6M); FQ3 Non-optical $325.6M (+51.7% YoY, HPC $106.7M); 9M HPC $207.7M
shifts.note: HPC (AI-related) within non-optical is compounding at $100M+ quarterly — this is liquid cooling and copper interconnect for AI racks, a secular tailwind independent of the optical upgrade cycle

shifts.trend: FQ2 gross margin 12.2% (essentially flat); FQ3 gross margin 11.9% (+20bps YoY); operating income +52.1% to $120M in FQ3
shifts.note: Fabrinet's margins are thin by design as a contract manufacturer; operating income outpacing revenue growth (+52% vs +39%) means operating leverage is present even on thin gross margins

FN.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating — DCI at +90% is the proof point that inter-DC AI fabric demand is real and FN is the manufacturing partner of choice for that wave; the HPC non-optical line adds a second AI-specific vector that wasn't visible two quarters ago

GEV.headline: Total RPO surged from $150.2B to $163.3B and Power RPO hit $99.7B, with HA-Turbine orders re-accelerating as Gas Power EBITDA margin expanded to 16.3% from 11.6% — pricing power is being captured as the order backlog converts

shifts.trend: FY25 total RPO $150.2B (+26% YoY); Power RPO $94.4B (+29%); Q1 2026 total RPO $163.3B (+32% YoY, +9% QoQ); Power RPO $99.7B (+30% YoY)
shifts.note: RPO compounding at 30%+ YoY while already at $150B+ is the key data point — the backlog duration and magnitude indicate hyperscaler and utility demand for gas turbines is multi-year structural, not cyclical

shifts.trend: FY25 heavy-duty gas turbine orders 110 units (vs 68 FY24, 41 FY23); HA-Turbines 43 (vs 25, 8); Q1 2026 HA-Turbine orders 12 (vs 8 Q1 2025), heavy-duty 28 (vs 29)
shifts.note: HA-Turbines (the highest-value units) orders re-accelerated in Q1 2026 after the FY25 surge; the order trajectory for HA-Turbines (8→25→43 FY23→24→25) is the strongest evidence of pricing-mix improvement

shifts.trend: FY25 Power segment EBITDA margin 14.7% (vs 12.5%); Q1 2026 Gas Power EBITDA margin 16.3% (vs 11.6% Q1 2025)
shifts.note: 470bps margin expansion in one quarter on favorable pricing and higher volume — the backlog pricing embedded in prior years' order signings is delivering as those units ship

shifts.trend: FY25: $5.3B acquisition of remaining 50% of Prolec GE (transformer JV) announced Oct 2025; Q1 2026: Prolec GE closed Feb 2026; Electrification RPO benefiting from Prolec + AC substation/HVDC demand
shifts.note: Prolec GE adds transformer manufacturing scale at exactly the moment when grid infrastructure demand is surging for data center power — a strategic bolt-on that extends GEV's total addressable market

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $38.1B (+9% organic); Q1 2026 revenue $9.3B (+16%); Q1 free cash flow $4.8B vs $1.0B prior year
shifts.note: Free cash flow quadrupled in Q1 2026 — partially from the Prolec GE acquisition gain but also from operating leverage as backlog converts at favorable pricing; this is the thesis materializing

GEV.verdict: The gas turbine thesis for data center power is converting from backlog to earnings — the $163B RPO and 470bps margin expansion confirm that GEV is capturing the pricing and volume upside simultaneously; Prolec GE extends the transformer/electrification angle

GNRC.headline: C&I segment inflected from +4.9% (FY25) to +28% (Q1 FY26) on data center demand, while residential softness abated — the backup power thesis strengthened as GNRC reorganized around Residential/C&I segments and acquired Enercon for hyperscale custom power

shifts.trend: FY25 C&I segment +4.9% on data center/industrial; Q1 FY26 C&I segment $510M (+28%) driven by global data center customers, industrial distributor/rental channels, controls solutions
shifts.note: The +28% C&I growth is a step-change from FY25's modest +5%; data center customers are now the explicit primary growth driver, not just a contributing factor

shifts.trend: FY25 total sales $4.21B (-2%); Q1 FY26 total sales $1.06B (+12% YoY); net income $73M (EPS $1.24) vs $44M ($0.73) Q1 FY25
shifts.note: Total company inflected from negative to positive growth as C&I more than offset residential softness; EPS growth of 70% YoY validates the operating leverage in the data center-exposed business

shifts.trend: FY25 adjusted EBITDA margin 17.0% (vs 18.4%); Q1 FY26 C&I segment margin 13.0% (vs 11.4%); Residential margin 25.1% (vs 20.3%); consolidated EBITDA +29%
shifts.note: Both segment margins expanded in Q1 FY26; the Residential margin expansion is on operational efficiencies; C&I margin expansion on price/cost and Allmand acquisition accretion

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: large-megawatt backup generator market 'significantly supply-constrained'; Q1 FY26: Enercon Engineering acquired for custom power equipment and industrial enclosures for hyperscale/enterprise data centers
shifts.note: Supply constraint is still cited as a key market characteristic — GNRC is capacity-adding through acquisitions (Enercon) and product line expansion to capture the constrained market opportunity

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: $500M new buyback authorized + launched large-megawatt diesel gensets; Q1 FY26: Enercon Engineering acquired; segment reorganization to Residential/C&I effective in Q1
shifts.note: The organizational realignment to C&I as a standalone segment signals management's strategic prioritization of the data center backup power market — it's being run as a distinct growth business

GNRC.verdict: The data center backup power thesis is now the primary driver of GNRC's growth, not a niche segment — the +28% C&I growth, Enercon acquisition, and segment reorganization confirm management is doubling down; the large-megawatt market being supply-constrained is the key upside lever

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud grew +63% in Q1 2026 (vs +36% FY25) and operating income tripled to $6.6B as RPO nearly doubled from $242.8B to $467.6B on multi-gigawatt TPU hardware supply agreements — cloud backlog now dwarfs the full-year revenue base

shifts.trend: Google Cloud FY25 revenue $58.7B (+36% YoY); Q1 2026 Google Cloud revenue $20.0B (+63% YoY)
shifts.note: A 27-point acceleration in Cloud revenue growth in one quarter is exceptional; the TPU hardware supply agreements and AI workload wins are converting to revenue faster than anticipated

shifts.trend: Google Cloud FY25 operating income $13.9B vs $6.1B FY24; Q1 2026 Cloud operating income $6.6B vs $2.2B Q1 2025 — 3x YoY
shifts.note: Cloud operating income growing 3x YoY while revenue grows 63% means margins are expanding dramatically; this confirms the AI infrastructure investment is generating high-margin workload revenue, not just capex burn

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $91.4B (vs $52.5B FY24, +74%); Q1 2026 capex $35.7B; FY26 guidance 'significantly increase' from $91.4B (implied $130B+)
shifts.note: Q1 2026 capex of $35.7B annualizes to ~$143B — the 'significantly increase' language is conservative relative to the actual run-rate; this is the largest capex commitment in GOOGL's history

shifts.trend: FY25 RPO $242.8B; Q1 2026 RPO $467.6B (vs $242.8B at Dec 31 2025) — 93% jump in one quarter; $462.3B is Google Cloud
shifts.note: Multi-gigawatt TPU hardware supply agreements are now booked in RPO — this is a structural shift where Google is selling compute capacity, not just cloud services; the revenue timing (majority in 2027) explains the RPO surge

shifts.trend: FY25: Wiz ($32B) and Intersect ($4.8B) acquisition commitments; Q1 2026: both closed ($29.5B and $5.9B actual); $40B committed to a private company (March 2026)
shifts.note: GOOGL is deploying massive capital into AI ecosystem bets — the $40B private company commitment dwarfs any single acquisition in its history; concentration in AI infrastructure and frontier AI is accelerating

GOOGL.verdict: Google Cloud is the most powerful demand signal in the dataset — tripling operating income while growing 63% confirms the inference stack is generating economics, not just spending; the $467B RPO with TPU hardware supply agreements means this is a 3-5 year demand lock-in

HPE.headline: Networking surged +151% in Q1 FY26 from full-quarter Juniper inclusion, but the Cloud & AI segment (servers + storage) contracted -2.7% as AI server margin dilution overwhelmed volumes, leaving the integration investment as a work-in-progress thesis

shifts.trend: FY25 Networking +51.1% (+$2.3B, $1.4B Juniper product post-7/2/25 close); Q1 FY26 Networking +151.5% (+$1.6B) to $2.7B as Juniper contributed full quarter
shifts.note: The Networking jump is almost entirely a Juniper comparison-period effect — base revenue only had 4 months of Juniper in Q1 FY25 vs a full quarter now; organic Networking growth needs to be parsed carefully

shifts.trend: FY25 GAAP operating loss $437M (Hybrid Cloud $1.6B impairment + $458M acquisition costs); Q1 FY26 gross margin 35.9% (+670bps YoY) on Juniper revenue mix; non-GAAP operating margin 12.7% (+280bps)
shifts.note: Juniper's software/services revenue mix is structurally margin-accretive vs prior HPE server-heavy mix; the gross margin jump is real and will persist as Juniper revenue normalizes within the combined company

shifts.trend: FY25 Server +10.2% YoY; Q1 FY26 Cloud & AI segment (new: Server + Hybrid Cloud + Financial Services) -2.7% to $6.4B; AI servers growing but GPU-heavy mix carries 'limited margins'
shifts.note: The server business is actually contracting on a total-segment basis despite AI server growth — legacy server demand is declining and AI server mix dilutes margins; this is the bear case for the server side of HPE

shifts.trend: FY25: ARR $3.15B (+63% YoY, majority Juniper); Q1 FY26: Juniper integration costs $123M + $23M restructuring; $600M synergy target by FY28 requires $800M investment over the period
shifts.note: Juniper integration is ahead in terms of revenue contribution but behind in synergy capture; $800M of investment needed to reach $600M synergies by FY28 is NPV-negative on its own — it depends on revenue upside from combined portfolio

shifts.trend: FY25: DOJ required Instant On and Mist AIOps divestiture as Juniper conditions; Q1 FY26: SCOTUS IEEPA ruling uncertainty; memory component shortages cited as new margin risk
shifts.note: Memory shortages (flagged explicitly in Q1 FY26) are a new near-term headwind — HPE buys memory for servers and supply tightness will either raise costs or constrain server volumes

HPE.verdict: The Juniper networking thesis is directionally correct but the server/Cloud & AI segment is the drag that makes the overall narrative complicated; the $1.6B goodwill impairment in FY25 was a warning — HPE is a portfolio rebalancing story, not a pure-play AI inference infrastructure winner

HUBB.headline: Datacenter-driven organic growth in Electrical Solutions accelerated from +7.1% in FY25 to +10.6% in Q1 FY26, while backlog expanded to $2.16B; tariff headwinds emerged as a margin offset

shifts.trend: Electrical Solutions organic growth +7.2% (FY25) → +10.6% (Q1 FY26); segment revenue $2.17B → $568M quarterly run-rate
shifts.note: Datacenter vertical explicitly cited as primary driver in both filings; acceleration is real, not mix-shift

shifts.trend: Total organic sales +3.3% (FY25) → +8.2% (Q1 FY26); total revenues $5.84B → $1.52B quarterly
shifts.note: Q1 acceleration reflects stronger datacenter pull-through into both Electrical and Utility segments

shifts.trend: Adjusted operating margin +80bps in FY25 to 22.5%; overall adjusted margin +110bps in Q1 FY26; Utility Solutions Q1 margin +190bps to 21.8%
shifts.note: Margin expansion broadening; pricing and productivity offsetting tariff/commodity inflation but Electrical Solutions Q1 margin squeezed -30bps

shifts.trend: Backlog $1.90B (YE24) → $2.16B (YE25); substantially all to ship within 12 months — lead times unchanged but demand outpacing supply
shifts.note: Backlog-to-revenue ratio suggests healthy but not excessive demand pipeline; no capacity constraint language yet

shifts.trend: $958M deployed on acquisitions in FY25 to expand product reach; Q1 FY26 no major acquisition disclosed
shifts.note: M&A-led capacity build in FY25 should increase addressable datacenter exposure in 2026

HUBB.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — datacenter organic growth is picking up speed into 2026, but tariff headwinds on Electrical Solutions margins deserve monitoring as a margin-cap risk

INTC.headline: DCAI revenue recovered to $5.1B (+22% YoY) in Q1 2026 on ASP-led premium mix, but supply constraints widened from Intel 7 in FY25 to substrates and memory in Q1, and Foundry losses remained near $2.4B

shifts.trend: DCAI revenue $14.3B (FY25) vs $13.5B (FY24); Q1 2026 DCAI $5.1B (+22% YoY) — quarterly run-rate already implies FY26 well above FY25
shifts.note: Volume -5% but ASP +27% on premium mix — demand-led, not capacity-led growth; supply is the actual constraint

shifts.trend: Intel 7 supply constraints emerged Q4 FY25 and persisted into Q1 2026; Q1 2026 additionally flagged substrate and memory component shortages expected to persist throughout 2026
shifts.note: Constraint set broadened — Intel's own fab plus third-party components; most severe in H1 2026 per guidance

shifts.trend: Intel Foundry external revenue $307M in FY25 vs Foundry total $17.8B (mostly intersegment); Q1 2026 Foundry external $174M only (now Altera external); Foundry operating loss $10.3B FY25 → $2.4B Q1 2026 alone
shifts.note: 18A ramp costs not yet absorbed by external revenue; Intel 14A paused if no customer — existential pivot risk to TSMC dependency

shifts.trend: Gross margin 39.4% in Q1 2026 (+2.5pts YoY); prior FY25 gross margin was structurally depressed by $10.3B Foundry losses
shifts.note: Products gross margins improving; consolidated margin recovery gated on whether Foundry losses shrink as 18A ramp matures

shifts.trend: GAAP operating loss $3.1B in Q1 2026 on $4.1B restructuring; FY25 SoftBank $2.0B + NVIDIA $5.0B equity injections; Apollo Ireland SCIP buyout in Q2 2026
shifts.note: Capital structure actions are dilutive and complex; NVIDIA partnership may shift competitive dynamics but adds strategic uncertainty

INTC.verdict: Thesis impaired — supply constraints widened rather than narrowed, Foundry losses persist at scale, and Intel's ability to self-fund 18A to competitive parity without a major external customer remains unproven

KLAC.headline: Revenue accelerated to a record $3.42B in Q3 (+11% YoY) from $3.30B in Q2 (+7%), with Korea HBM/DRAM spend surging +80% as China stabilized; gross margin dipped slightly on DRAM chip input cost headwind

shifts.trend: Revenue $3.30B Q2 FY26 (+7% YoY) → $3.42B Q3 FY26 (+11% YoY); nine-month revenue pace at record
shifts.note: Acceleration driven by HBM/DRAM investment surge — core to the inference stack bottleneck thesis

shifts.trend: Korea revenue +34% YoY in Q2 → +80% YoY in Q3 ($681M); memory DRAM/HBM cited as exclusive driver in both quarters
shifts.note: HBM capacity investment is accelerating, not plateauing — KLAC process control is a direct beneficiary

shifts.trend: China $995M (30.2%) Q2 → $830M (24.2%) Q3; nine-month China flat at $3.09B — export controls capping incremental growth
shifts.note: China normalizing at 24-30% range; not collapsing but not growing — export controls are a structural ceiling

shifts.trend: Gross margin 61.4% in Q2 → 61.1% in Q3; DRAM chip costs for image computers rising; mgmt guided this continues in CY2026
shifts.note: Transitory per mgmt, but escalating DRAM input costs are a structural negative as long as HBM pricing stays elevated

shifts.trend: Q2 returns $548M repurchases + $250M dividends; Q3 returns $626M repurchases + $249M dividends + $7B added to buyback authorization
shifts.note: Returning capital aggressively signals mgmt confidence in cash generation durability

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact — HBM-driven DRAM investment surge is the clearest read-through for the inference memory bottleneck; DRAM input cost drag on margins is real but manageable and labeled transitory

LEU.headline: Centrus transitioned from DOE HALEU task-order win ($900M) in January 2026 to signing the Fluor EPC contract and announcing Oklo HALEU JV by March, building out the full supply chain; backlog grew to $3.9B as advanced technology costs surged +530%

shifts.trend: Total backlog $3.8B (YE25) → $3.9B (Q1 FY26); LEU contingent contracts grew $2.3B → $2.4B; Q1 revenue $76.7M (+5% YoY)
shifts.note: Backlog growth is contingent on capacity build — revenue will lag until Piketon centrifuges come online (2029 target)

shifts.trend: Advanced technology costs $3.0M (Q1 FY25) → $18.9M (Q1 FY26, +530%); Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing started Dec 2025 ($560M+ multi-year); Fluor EPC signed Feb 2026
shifts.note: Spend acceleration reflects transition from planning to execution; cash burn will rise sharply before revenue follows

shifts.trend: SWU spot price held $200/unit vs $34 historic low in 2018; Russian supply still 43% of global enrichment — non-Russian market remains structurally undersupplied in both filings
shifts.note: Market structure supports LEU pricing and Centrus's backlog conversion; geopolitical risk also cuts both ways

shifts.trend: Technical Solutions $21.8M (Q1 FY25) → $32.1M (Q1 FY26, +47%); HALEU Operation Contract HOC volumes driving growth
shifts.note: HALEU deconversion JV with Oklo (March 2026) extends Technical Solutions addressable market to a new commercial segment that does not yet exist

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: DOE task order won $900M; Q1 FY26: FY27 DOE budget proposal does not include HALEU Operation Contract cascade funding
shifts.note: Congressional appropriation risk is the single biggest overhang — Centrus's HALEU expansion thesis is entirely publicly funded in the near term

LEU.verdict: Thesis intact but execution-gated — the HALEU supply chain is assembling faster than expected (Fluor EPC, Oklo JV), but FY27 DOE funding uncertainty and a 2029 commercial ramp mean the upside is long-dated

LITE.headline: Revenue surged from $665.5M (+65.5% YoY) to $808.4M (+90.1% YoY) as supply allocation hardened and gross margin expanded a further 810bps to 44.2%, confirming LITE as the InP/EML choke point for the 1.6T transceiver cycle

shifts.trend: Revenue $665.5M Q2 FY26 (+65.5% YoY) → $808.4M Q3 FY26 (+90.1% YoY); 9-month revenue $2.01B (+72.4%)
shifts.note: Growth rate itself is accelerating — not a base-effect artifact; cloud transceiver volume plus OCS ramp driving sequential step-up

shifts.trend: Gross margin 36.1% Q2 FY26 (+1,130bps YoY) → 44.2% Q3 FY26 (+1,540bps YoY); 9-month GM 38.8% vs prior-year 25.8%
shifts.note: Structural re-rating, not one-quarter noise — supply allocation allows LITE to optimize customer mix; 200G-lane ASP uplift is durable while 1.6T ramps

shifts.trend: Q2: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation'; Q3 language hardened to 'has required us to make decisions on supply allocation'
shifts.note: Verb tense shift signals constraint is deepening, not easing; manufacturing expansion in progress but lagging demand

shifts.trend: Top 2 customers 41% combined in Q2 FY26 (24% + 17%) → 38% combined in Q3 FY26 (26% + 12%) — top customer growing share, second shrinking
shifts.note: Concentration risk remains elevated; top customer growing its share to 26% introduces hyperscaler dependency risk

shifts.trend: OCS revenue >$10M in Q2 FY26 → >$25M in Q3 FY26; >$38M nine-month cumulative — optical circuit switches accelerating off zero
shifts.note: OCS is a new revenue line that didn't exist a year ago; early but confirms LITE's systems diversification thesis alongside laser chips

LITE.verdict: Thesis unlocked and upgrading — LITE is demonstrably the InP supply bottleneck for the 1.6T cycle, and both growth and margin expansion are accelerating simultaneously; the primary risk is execution on manufacturing scale

LRCX.headline: DRAM/HBM investment surge drove memory mix from 34% to 39% of revenue and lifted quarterly revenue 9% sequentially to $5.84B, while China held at 34% and Korea jumped to 23% — inference-stack memory capex rotation is the dominant story

shifts.trend: Revenue $5.34B Q2 FY26 (flat QoQ) → $5.84B Q3 FY26 (+9% QoQ); nine-month revenue $16.51B vs $13.26B prior year (+24%)
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration driven entirely by DRAM customer investments — direct read-through to HBM capacity buildout

shifts.trend: Memory mix 34% in Q2 → 39% in Q3; Foundry 59% → 54%; DRAM-specific demand cited as exclusive driver of memory increase
shifts.note: Rotation from mature foundry to DRAM/HBM is a secular shift aligned with inference memory bottleneck thesis

shifts.trend: China 35% of Dec 2025 revenue → 34% of Mar 2026 revenue; Q2 already down from 43% in Sep 2025 quarter
shifts.note: China stabilizing in the 34-35% range; export restrictions contained downside but not eliminating the base

shifts.trend: Gross margin 49.6% Q2 → 49.8% Q3; six-month at 50.0% vs 47.7% prior year
shifts.note: Margin stable despite customer mix shifts; better factory efficiencies offsetting geographic mix changes

shifts.trend: Q3 returns $1.16B repurchases + $326M dividends + $751M debt paydown; cash dropped $4.77B from $6.20B
shifts.note: Aggressive capital return; debt paydown alongside buybacks suggests balance sheet discipline

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — DRAM/HBM investment is inflecting, driving Lam's sequential revenue acceleration; the mix shift to memory is the clearest single-quarter confirmation of the inference memory bottleneck

META.headline: FY26 capex guidance raised a second time to $125-145B (from $115-135B at year-end, from $72.2B actual in FY25), with $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments locked in — demand signal for the entire inference stack is unambiguously accelerating

shifts.trend: Capex $72.2B (FY25) → guided $115-135B (Jan 2026) → raised to $125-145B (Apr 2026); Q1 FY26 capex alone $19.8B (+74% YoY annualized)
shifts.note: Two upward revisions within a single quarter signals demand surprise rather than pre-planned buildout; mid-point $135B implies 87% YoY growth

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $201.0B (+22%); Q1 FY26 $56.3B (+33%); ad impressions growth +12% (FY25) → +19% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Core business acceleration provides FCF fuel; Q1 operating cash flow $32.2B single-quarter

shifts.trend: Non-cancelable commitments $237.7B at Mar 31 2026; $42.3B due 2026, $47.7B in 2027; April 2026 added another $24B in multi-year infra contracts post-quarter-end
shifts.note: Commitments of this scale lock in demand for GPU, HBM, power, cooling, and networking suppliers for multiple years

shifts.trend: Reality Labs operating loss $4.03B Q1 FY26; FY25 full-year loss $19.2B; FY26 expected 'similar to 2025'
shifts.note: RL losses are a drag but stable and guided — not a new risk; core FoA profitability absorbs the burn

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 operating income $22.9B (+30%) on $56.3B revenue; margin ~40% despite capex surge; FY25 FoA operating income $102.5B (+18%)
shifts.note: Revenue growth outpacing capex ramp in the near term; margin sustainability depends on AI monetization materializing

META.verdict: Thesis unlocked — META's capex escalation is the single most important demand signal for the inference stack; the two upward revisions within months confirm AI infrastructure is a structural multi-year commitment, not a cyclical bet

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions data center revenue growth stepped up from +$67M to +$130M sequentially as Modine announced a Reverse Morris Trust to spin Performance Technologies, positioning as a pure-play datacenter cooling company despite margin still compressed by ramp inefficiencies

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions data center quarterly growth +$67.4M (Q2 FY26) → +$130.0M (Q3 FY26); segment revenue $454.4M → $544.6M (+51% YoY Q3)
shifts.note: Growth rate nearly doubled sequentially — hyperscale North America and Europe customers cited in both filings

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions operating margin 17.6% (Q2 FY25 comparison base) → 13.7% (Q2 FY26) → 15.3% (Q3 FY26); ramp inefficiencies cited both quarters
shifts.note: Margin recovering sequentially but still 200bps below year-ago; capacity ramp is the key variable — margins should normalize as utilization fills

shifts.trend: Announced incremental $100M datacenter capex (Q2 FY26) → incremental manufacturing lease signed (Q3 FY26); 1H capex $59.4M (+$19.1M YoY)
shifts.note: Continuous capacity additions signal mgmt confidence in demand durability; lease model lowers upfront capital risk

shifts.trend: January 2026: Reverse Morris Trust announced to spin Performance Technologies into Gentherm; MOD retains Climate Solutions only; transaction value ~$1.0B, $210M cash at close
shifts.note: Corporate restructuring is a thesis catalyst — pure-play datacenter cooling focus unlocks better multiple; not yet closed (targeted YE CY26)

MOD.verdict: Thesis intact and catalyzed — the RMT spin is a structural unlocking event that repositions MOD as a pure-play datacenter thermal management company; execution on margin recovery from ramp inefficiencies is the remaining gating item

MRVL.headline: Annual data center revenue reached $6.1B (+46% YoY, 74% of mix) and Marvell closed Celestial AI and XConn acquisitions post-year-end, adding photonic fabric interconnect and CXL/PCIe switching to the custom ASIC platform ahead of the next silicon cycle

shifts.trend: Data center Q3 FY26 $1.52B (+38% YoY) → FY26 full-year $6.1B (+46% YoY); total revenue $2.07B Q3 → $8.2B FY26 (+42% YoY)
shifts.note: Full-year data center growth outpaced the already-strong Q3 quarterly rate — momentum into FY27 is building

shifts.trend: Gross margin 51.6% in Q3 FY26 → 51.0% FY26 full-year (vs 23.0%/41.3% in prior-year comps distorted by impairments); structural GM now tracking to ~51%
shifts.note: Prior-year comparisons were impaired by $357M restructuring charge; underlying ~51% gross margin reflects high-ASP custom ASIC mix shift

shifts.trend: Celestial AI acquired Feb 2, 2026 (~$1.3B cash + shares) for photonic fabric; XConn acquired Feb 10, 2026 ($280M + shares) for CXL/PCIe switching — neither was in Q3 results
shifts.note: Two acquisitions closed within 10 days post-year-end extend silicon photonics and CXL capabilities — directly relevant to CPO and scale-up interconnect

shifts.trend: Distributor A (hyperscaler conduit) 38% of Q3 revenue → 37% of FY26; Direct Customer A 11% Q3 → 14% FY26; combined concentration stable but top-heavy
shifts.note: Top customer concentration at 51% (distributor + direct) remains the key single-customer risk if hyperscaler ASIC program pauses

shifts.trend: China shipment destination 40% of Q3 revenue (mostly non-China end-customers); FY26 10-K flags ongoing US export restrictions and risk of Chinese customer stockpiling or indigenous supplier substitution
shifts.note: China exposure is routing risk, not end-demand risk — but incremental restrictions could force customer redesigns

MRVL.verdict: Thesis intact and extending — FY26 confirmed MRVL as the AI custom ASIC + electro-optics platform leader; Celestial AI and XConn acquisitions add photonic fabric and CXL to the stack, positioning for the next-generation inference interconnect cycle

MSFT.headline: Azure growth held at 39-40% for two consecutive quarters while commercial RPO nearly doubled to $627B and Q3 capex reached $30.9B — hyperscaler infrastructure demand locked in for multiple years

shifts.trend: Azure +39% (Q2 FY26) → +40% (Q3 FY26); Microsoft Cloud $51.5B (Q2) → $54.5B (Q3) — 5% sequential growth, 26-29% YoY
shifts.note: Azure growth stabilizing in the 39-40% band, not decelerating; reacceleration from 39% to 40% is directionally positive

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 capex $29.9B (+89% YoY); Q3 FY26 capex $30.9B; 9M total $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year (+96%)
shifts.note: Capex run-rate implies FY26 full-year well above $100B — persistent demand signal for entire inference infrastructure stack

shifts.trend: Commercial RPO $625B (Q2 FY26, +110% YoY) → $627B (Q3 FY26, +99% YoY); total RPO $631B Dec → Q3 not separately broken out
shifts.note: RPO doubling YoY indicates customers are locking in multi-year capacity — forward visibility for MSFT and for its hardware suppliers is exceptionally strong

shifts.trend: Microsoft Cloud gross margin 67% (Q2, compressed by AI infra) → reported Q3 cloud gross margin not separately disclosed but operating income +20% on +18% revenue
shifts.note: Operating leverage improving despite capex surge; AI monetization through Copilot/M365 beginning to offset infrastructure cost ramp

MSFT.verdict: Thesis intact and compounding — RPO nearly doubling and capex at $30B/quarter locks in multi-year demand for the entire inference stack; Azure growth stability at 40% removes the deceleration risk that was latent in Q2

MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

MXL.headline: Infrastructure (optical DSP) revenue began recovering with Q1 2026 +$36.3M YoY after FY25's +$34.3M full-year gain, and customer concentration broadened significantly — but no hyperscale optical DSP revenue break-out limits visibility into the 1.6T cycle timing

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $467.6M (+30% YoY); Q1 2026 revenue $137.2M (+43% YoY); infrastructure sub-segment +$34.3M in FY25 → +$36.3M in a single quarter in Q1 2026
shifts.note: Infrastructure quarterly growth now matching the full-year FY25 gain — optical DSP and wireless backhaul traction accelerating

shifts.trend: Top customer >20% of revenue (FY25 implied) → 13% (Q1 2026); top 10 = 65% (FY25) → 56% (Q1 2026)
shifts.note: Customer base broadening suggests recovery is broad-based, not single-customer-driven — reduces idiosyncratic risk

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 57% (+3pts YoY); Q1 2026 gross margin not separately disclosed but volume-led growth with no price headwinds cited
shifts.note: Gross margin trajectory positive in FY25; Q1 mix shift toward higher-margin infrastructure should sustain or improve this

shifts.trend: Broadband segment drove FY25 recovery (+$87.6M, +59%); Q1 2026 broadband only +$2.7M — broadband recovery largely complete
shifts.note: The FY25 broadband bounce is a one-time uplift; forward growth must come from infrastructure/optical — exactly where the 1.6T opportunity sits

MXL.verdict: Thesis intact but low-visibility — MXL is positioned for the 1.6T optical DSP cycle and the recovery is accelerating, but without explicit hyperscale optical DSP revenue disclosure, the magnitude of the opportunity is hard to size

NOW.headline: Subscription revenue growth held steady at 21-22% YoY and RPO grew from $28.2B to $27.7B (with cRPO +23%), but the inference stack thesis depends on whether AI agent adoption via Now Assist drives consumption acceleration that isn't yet visible in these metrics

shifts.trend: FY25 subscription revenue $12.9B (+21%); Q1 2026 subscription $3.67B (+22%); total revenue $13.28B FY25 → $3.77B Q1 2026
shifts.note: Growth rate stable at 21-22% — neither inflecting up nor decelerating; consistent execution rather than AI-driven step-change yet

shifts.trend: RPO $28.2B (Dec 31 2025, +27% YoY) → $27.7B (Mar 31 2026, +25% YoY); cRPO +25% FY25 → +23% Q1 2026 — slight moderation
shifts.note: RPO sequentially declined $500M QoQ as billings growth moderates; cRPO growth deceleration bears watching

shifts.trend: Now Assist broadly deployed across ITSM/CSM/HRSD/SecOps in FY25; Q1 2026 consumption pricing for AI agents in place across product lines
shifts.note: Consumption-based AI agent pricing is the monetization lever — not yet material in reported revenue but the mechanism is live

shifts.trend: US federal self-hosted revenue $83M incremental FY25 vs FY24; Q1 2026 professional services $99M (+19%) — diversified revenue composition held
shifts.note: Federal on-prem deployments growing as agencies prefer non-cloud AI agent deployments

NOW.verdict: Thesis intact but dependent on AI inflection — ServiceNow is executing cleanly on its core SaaS growth, but the inference-stack angle requires AI agent consumption to drive a visible revenue step-up that hasn't yet materialized in the filings

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66–68% YoY; customer concentration formally disclosed at 4-way >10%; China is now effectively zero, but supply/energy commentary unchanged.

shifts.note: Slight re-acceleration on a full-year basis after Blackwell ramp
shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp

shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependence newly visible — diligence risk-additive
shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22/15/13/11%); 10-K did not previously break this out

shifts.note: China optionality fully impaired across both filings
shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within target

shifts.note: Quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.trend: Both filings cite data-center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint on customer deployments — no easing language added

shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF translation
shifts.trend: FY26 10-K disclosed $17.5B in private/infra investments plus $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees to early-stage AI customers; Q3 added $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26 Q4–FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. The new disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis; China optionality is now fully impaired and should be modeled at zero.

NVT.headline: Systems Protection organic growth accelerated from ~17% (FY25) to +50.1% (Q1 FY26) as EPG acquisition bedded in, driving segment margin +220bps to 22.7%; gross margin squeezed -290bps by tariffs and capacity investment across both periods

shifts.trend: Systems Protection organic +~17% (FY25, ~16pts from infrastructure) → +50.1% organic (Q1 FY26, ~46pts from infrastructure/data centers); segment revenue $2.59B FY25 → $894.8M Q1 run-rate
shifts.note: Infrastructure contribution to organic growth tripled — data center demand is the dominant driver pulling through enclosures and liquid cooling

shifts.trend: Systems Protection segment margin 20.7% (FY25) → 22.7% (Q1 FY26, +220bps); segment income +95% on +76% revenue — operating leverage evident
shifts.note: Operating leverage in Systems Protection is inflecting positively despite tariff headwinds — the EPG integration is adding scale

shifts.trend: Gross margin 37.7% (FY25, -2.5pts) → 35.9% (Q1 FY26, -290bps YoY); tariffs and raw materials cited in both filings
shifts.note: Consolidated gross margin contracting despite segment improvement — mix from Electrical Connections (lower margin) and tariff pass-through delay

shifts.trend: Backlog $2.3B at YE25 (majority to ship 2026); Q1 FY26 EPG contributed $137.7M incremental — backlog composition not separately restated
shifts.note: Backlog provides 6-12 month revenue visibility; supply capacity is the execution risk as data center orders accelerate

shifts.trend: ~$383M returned FY25 ($253M repurchases + $130M dividends); Q1 FY26 dividend raised to $0.21/share; $146.9M remaining on authorization
shifts.note: Capital return program maintained alongside EPG acquisition integration — balance sheet discipline intact

NVT.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating — NVT's enclosures and liquid cooling business is the direct beneficiary of the data center power infrastructure buildout, and Q1 organic growth acceleration to 50%+ confirms the thesis is moving from narrative to numbers

OKLO.headline: Oklo signed the first hyperscaler prepayment agreement (Meta, 1.2 GW) in January 2026 and secured DOE Aurora Safety Design approval in February, converting the LOI-stage pipeline into binding commitments while cash burn is guided to $80-100M operating + $350-450M capex for FY26

shifts.trend: Order book ~18,100 MWe at Sept 2025 (Q3 FY25) → Meta prepayment agreement 1.2 GW signed Jan 5, 2026 (FY25 10-K); first binding hyperscaler prepayment secured
shifts.note: Transition from non-binding LOIs to binding prepayment with Meta is a qualitative step-change in commitment certainty

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25: NRC Phase I pre-application readiness assessment complete, no significant gaps; PDC topical report accepted Sept 2025. FY25 10-K: Aurora Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approved by DOE Feb 2026
shifts.note: Regulatory de-risking is progressing on schedule; NRC license pathway clear as of Q3, DOE design approval in Feb 2026 — first commercial deployment 2028 target intact

shifts.trend: 9M FY25 investing cash outflows $605.9M; FY26 guided $350-450M capex + $80-100M operating cash burn — total cash deployment guided $430-550M for FY26
shifts.note: Accelerating cash deployment into Pike County land purchase, Tennessee fuel center, and manufacturing — balance sheet $1.18B at Sept 2025 supports near-term runway

shifts.trend: Operating expenses $52.8M FY24 → $139.3M FY25 (+164%); 9M FY25 opex $82.2M vs $52.8M FY24 full-year
shifts.note: Opex burn reflects headcount ramp (205 employees at YE25) and project development; pre-revenue company, burn rate is the primary financial metric

OKLO.verdict: Thesis intact and de-risking — Meta's binding prepayment and DOE safety design approval in the same quarter convert Oklo from a story stock to a company with hyperscaler revenue committed and regulatory pathway confirmed; 2028 deployment is now the primary execution variable

ORCL.headline: OCI infrastructure revenue growth accelerated from +68% ($4.08B) in Q2 to +84% ($4.89B) in Q3, and RPO climbed from $523.3B to $552.6B — but 9-month capex of $39.2B (3x prior year) and $47.7B in new debt signal the buildout is outrunning near-term cash generation

shifts.trend: Cloud infrastructure revenue $4.08B Q2 FY26 (+68% YoY) → $4.89B Q3 FY26 (+84% YoY); sequential acceleration of +16pts in growth rate
shifts.note: Acceleration in OCI growth is the central data point for the inference infrastructure demand thesis; AI workloads displacing legacy on-prem Oracle DB

shifts.trend: RPO $523.3B (Nov 2025, vs $97.3B YoY = 5.4x) → $552.6B (Feb 2026, vs $130.2B YoY = 4.2x); RPO growing sequentially even as year-ago base normalized
shifts.note: RPO at this scale ($552B) represents multi-decade committed revenue; the magnitude implies AI/sovereign cloud contracts dominating the pipeline

shifts.trend: H1 FY26 capex $20.5B vs $6.3B H1 FY25 (3.3x); 9M FY26 capex $39.2B vs $12.1B 9M FY25 (3.2x); trend to continue 'next few fiscal years'
shifts.note: Capex is compounding, not plateauing — each quarter adds more than the prior year's entire H1

shifts.trend: Issued $17.9B senior notes Sept 2025 (Q2); $42.7B total senior notes + $5B mandatory convert by Q3 FY26; debt-funded capex at scale
shifts.note: Oracle is levering up aggressively to fund the OCI buildout; interest burden and debt covenants become a risk if OCI revenue ramp slows

shifts.trend: Cloud and software segment expenses +49% (Q2 FY26); 9M operating margin declining as infrastructure ramp hits income statement before revenue scales
shifts.note: Margin compression is the expected J-curve effect of an infrastructure build; OCI gross margins will eventually recover as utilization fills

ORCL.verdict: Thesis intact and high-conviction — Oracle's OCI infrastructure growth rate is accelerating into the 80%+ range, and RPO scale ($552B) provides extraordinary forward visibility; the leverage build-up is the key watch item but is consistent with a deliberate capacity-grab strategy

PLTR.headline: Revenue growth re-accelerated from +53% (FY25) to +85% (Q1 2026) with operating margin expanding from 31% to 46% as commercial revenue (+95%) overtook government (+76%) — AIP is generating compounding operating leverage

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $4.5B (+53% YoY); Q1 2026 $1.63B (+85% YoY) — quarterly run-rate implies FY26 well above $6B
shifts.note: Growth acceleration from 53% to 85% is a step-change, not a base effect; commercial and government both accelerating simultaneously

shifts.trend: FY25 operating income $1.41B (31% margin); Q1 2026 operating income $754M (46% margin) — 15pt margin expansion in one quarter
shifts.note: Operating leverage is the thesis validator — high-margin software scaling at 85% growth with 46% operating margin is exceptional

shifts.trend: FY25 commercial $2.07B (+60%); Q1 2026 commercial $774M (+95%); commercial now nearly matching government ($858M) in a single quarter
shifts.note: Commercial velocity overtaking government signals AIP adoption is broadening beyond defense/intelligence to enterprise — secular not cyclical

shifts.trend: FY25 US 74% of revenue; Q1 2026 US 79% (+5pts) — US concentration deepened; no single customer >10% in either period
shifts.note: No single-customer concentration risk but US geographic concentration increasing; international remains underpenetrated

shifts.trend: Gross margin 87% Q1 2026 vs 80% Q1 2025; contribution margins both segments 73% vs 61-62% — structural expansion
shifts.note: Gross margin expansion alongside revenue acceleration means Palantir is past the investment phase and into compounding returns

PLTR.verdict: Thesis intact and upgrading — Palantir is demonstrating that AIP as an inference orchestration layer generates compounding operating leverage at scale; the 46% operating margin at 85% growth is the strongest proof point yet that the AI software layer is being monetized

PWR.headline: Backlog surged from $43.98B (YE25) to $48.47B (+10.2% in a single quarter) and RPO reached $26.24B as datacenter-driven utility infrastructure demand accelerated from FY25's +20% revenue growth into Q1 FY26's +26%

shifts.trend: FY25 revenues $28.5B (+20.3%); Q1 FY26 revenues $7.87B (+26.3% YoY) — quarterly run-rate implies 2026 pace above FY25
shifts.note: Acceleration driven by Electric segment as datacenter-driven grid upgrade and interconnection work compounds
Backlog and RPO growing faster than revenue — demand is outpacing PWR's ability to execute; capacity is the constraint, not demand

shifts.trend: FY25 Electric segment margin 10.3% (flat YoY); Q1 FY26 overall operating margin 4.3% vs 3.8% (+50bps); gross margin 14.1% vs 13.4% (+70bps)
shifts.note: Margin improvement in Q1 despite SG&A surge from acquisitions — operating leverage beginning to show in the topline

shifts.trend: FY25: CEI acquisition driving critical-path datacenter electrical; DSI acquired July 2025 for mechanical/plumbing in tech load centers. Q1 FY26: both cited as primary growth catalysts for Electric and Underground segments
shifts.note: Acquisitions are additive and fully integrated into the datacenter narrative — Quanta is building a turnkey DC infrastructure capability

shifts.trend: FY25 $3.30B on acquisitions/investments; Q1 FY26 SG&A +$127M from acquisition integration; no new large acquisition announced in Q1
shifts.note: Capital deployment shifted from M&A to organic execution in Q1 — integration cadence rather than new deals

PWR.verdict: Thesis intact and strongest evidence yet — Quanta's $48B backlog growing 10% in a single quarter is the clearest supply-bottleneck signal in the grid/datacenter interconnection layer; the demand is booked, execution is the only gating item

SNOW.headline: Full-year FY26 revenue $4.7B (+31%) closed ahead of the Q3 pace, customers with >$1M ARR jumped 27% to 733, and NRR held at 125% — consistent execution but no AI-driven consumption step-up yet visible in the metrics

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 product revenue $1.16B (+29%); FY26 full-year product revenue ~$4.5B (+31%); consistent 29-31% range across the window
shifts.note: Growth rate stable — no inflection from AI workload adoption but also no deceleration despite macro uncertainty
